name: Alabama Mine
short_description: BLM public land near Penryn offering recreational gold panning and quartz collection in the historic Sierra Nevada foothills. No permit required; 25 lb daily limit applies.
description:
The Alabama Mine area sits within Placer County's rich gold country, where the 1849 Gold Rush left behind countless claims and mineralized zones. This BLM-managed location offers casual rockhounders easy access to metamorphic rocks and quartz veins typical of the Sierra Nevada foothills. The site lies at gentle elevation (564 ft) on rolling terrain, making it accessible for day trips from the Sacramento area.

Geologically, this region was shaped by Cretaceous granitic intrusions and metamorphic host rocks that host quartz veins and disseminated gold mineralization. The combination of weathered granitic material and exposed quartz outcrops makes this an ideal spot for small-scale collecting. You will find white to milky quartz specimens, mica flakes, and feldspathic material scattered across exposed hillsides and wash areas.

Expect a relaxed, low-pressure collecting environment where you can spend a few hours turning over rocks and checking quartz seams. The terrain supports both surface collecting and shallow hand-digging in exposed zones. Bring a small classifier or pan if you are interested in sampling for fine gold flakes, though this is more of a educational exercise than a profitable venture.

The area is popular with local weekend rockhounders and offers a good introduction to Sierra foothills geology. Nearby Penryn is a rural community, so plan accordingly for supplies and services.
history: The Alabama Mine area lies within the Mother Lode region of the Sierra Nevada foothills, where mid-Cretaceous granitic plutons intruded into metamorphic bedrock, creating quartz veins and zones of gold mineralization. This productive zone was heavily prospected during and after the 1849 Gold Rush, and weathering of primary quartz veins has left abundant secondary quartz and feldspar material across the landscape.
tips: Focus your search on exposed quartz outcrops and weathered hillsides where white quartz veins contrast with darker host rock. Hand tools like a small pick or pry bar work well for extracting quartz specimens from seams; a classifier and pan will help you spot fine gold in quartz-rich wash material. Look for milky white to clear quartz chunks 1-4 inches across, and scan mica-rich areas for flake samples. Stay within the marked BLM boundaries and do not disturb any old mining infrastructure or claim markers.
terrain_notes: Gentle sloping hillsides at 564 feet elevation with exposed bedrock, scattered quartz veins, and seasonal wash zones. Vegetation is sparse oak and chaparral typical of the foothills. Parking is on a dirt pullout near the site; walking distance to productive collecting areas is typically under 0.5 miles. Footing is rocky but not steep; sturdy footwear is recommended.
written_directions: From Penryn, take California State Route 49 (Gold Country Road) north toward Newcastle. Continue approximately 3 miles and watch for a dirt pullout on the east side of the road marked with a small BLM recreation sign. Park here and walk upslope to the east where quartz outcrops and weathered mining areas are visible. GPS coordinates: 38.844593, -121.157462.
road_conditions: Paved highway access (CA 49). Dirt pullout parking area suitable for passenger vehicles.
parking_notes: Small unpaved pullout near the site entrance. Space for 3-5 vehicles. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No removal of artifacts or disturbance of old mining infrastructure. Respect all claim markers and stay within BLM-open zones.
rules:

nearest_services: Penryn, approximately 3 miles south. Limited services; plan to bring water and supplies from larger towns like Rocklin (8 miles) or Auburn (12 miles).
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Gentle slope and short walking distances make this suitable for families and those with limited mobility. Dirt parking pullout is accessible by passenger vehicle. No paved trails; footing is rocky but not steep. Shade is minimal; bring sun protection and water.

faq.question: Can you find gold at Alabama Mine in California?
faq.answer: Yes. The Alabama Mine area sits in the historic Sierra Nevada Mother Lode zone where quartz veins host small amounts of gold. Fine gold flakes can be found in quartz-rich wash material and around weathered outcrops. Use a small pan or classifier to sample quartz debris. Most finds are specimen-grade rather than profitable, but it is an educational and fun way to learn about gold mineralization in the foothills.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Alabama Mine free and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Alabama Mine is completely free and requires no permit. The site is on BLM public land open for recreational collecting. You only need to follow the 25 lb per day personal use limit and avoid any marked mining claims or artifacts.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Penryn?
faq.answer: October through May is ideal. Cooler temperatures and occasional rain keep the foothills pleasant and help soften weathered material for easier hand collecting. Summer heat can exceed 95 degrees F, making midday collecting uncomfortable. Avoid the rainy winter months if you dislike muddy terrain, but late fall and early spring offer perfect conditions.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect quartz at Alabama Mine?
faq.answer: A small pry bar, cold chisel, or rock pick helps extract quartz specimens from seams. Bring gloves to protect your hands from sharp quartz edges and hot sun. A small classifier, gold pan, and magnifying glass are useful if you want to sample for fine gold and mica flakes. A backpack, water bottle, and sun hat are essential for a comfortable day.

faq.question: Is Alabama Mine good for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Absolutely. The site offers gentle terrain, short walking distances, and easy hand collecting with minimal equipment. Children as young as 6 can participate with supervision. The relaxed pace and accessible geology make it perfect for learning about Sierra Nevada rocks and California gold country without technical difficulty or hazard.

faq.question: How far is Alabama Mine from Sacramento and Rocklin?
faq.answer: Alabama Mine is approximately 45 minutes from Sacramento and 15 minutes from Rocklin via California State Route 49. It is an easy day trip for the greater Sacramento area. Nearby Penryn offers basic services; larger towns like Auburn and Newcastle have more amenities.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Penryn. Collect quartz, mica, and gold in the Sierra Nevada foothills. No permit. 25 lb daily limit. Perfect for beginners.
